coredata.dc:title: Orange-emissive carbon dot phosphors for warm white light-emitting diodes with high color rendering index
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Lin
Zheng, Jingxia
Du, Qin
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
Xu, Bingshe
coredata.dc:description: Orange-emissive carbon dot (o-CDs) phosphors were prepared to reduce the correlated color temperature and increase the color rendering index of carbon dots (CDs)-based white light-emitting diode (WLED) devices. 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene was selected as the carbon source and N, N-dimethylformamide was used as the nitrogen source to synthesize nitrogen doped o-CDs with wide and double-peak emission spectrum. Warm WLED device was designed by taking o-CDs as phosphors and excited by a UV chip. Results showed that o-CDs have a fluorescence quantum yield of 26.03%, their emission spectrum is wide enough to range from 480 to 680 nm, and show good photo and thermal stability, which is quite suitable for warm WLED phosphors. o-CDs based WLED device emits warm white light with a color coordinate of (0.41, 0.39) and a correlated color temperature of 3330 K. Compared with the current warm WLEDs devices based on CDs, their color rendering index reaches a high level, which is 91. This study provides a new idea for broadening the application of CDs in warm WLEDs that used in indoor lighting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Orange-emissive
Carbon dots
Warm WLEDs
High color rendering index
Indoor lighting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of o-CDs based WLED devices
Structure and elemental composition of o-CDs
Application of o-CDs in warm WLED

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51803148 , 51972221 , U1710117 and U1607120 ), Shanxi Provincial Youth Fund Project ( 201801D221180 , 201901D211501 ), Shanxi Provincial Excellent Talents Science and Technology Innovation Project ( 201805D211001 ) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of o-CDs before and after dialysis.
(a) XPS spectra of o-CDs and high resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s.
Illustration of chemical formation of o-CDs.
(a) TEM image of o-CDs (inset is the size distribution) and (b) HRTEM image of o-CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (b) PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths, inset: photograph of o-CDs under UV light.
(a) QY measurement of o-CDs and (b) time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of o-CDs.
Spectral composition of o-CDs under 360 nm excitation.
(a) PL spectra changes of o-CDs under continuous UV (λex = 365 nm) radiation and (b) dependence of PL intensity on radiation time for o-CDs under 365 nm.
(a) TG curve of o-CDs (N2 atmosphere, heating rate: 10 °C/min) and (b) a comparison of PL properties of o-CDs before and after heated at 120 °C.
(a) Emission spectra and (b) CIE chromaticity coordinates of the white LED under 3.4 V working voltage (inset: digital photo of LEDs).
(a) The emission spectra of different concentration of o-CDs and KH-792 in ethanol and (b) the photograph of o-CDs powder under daylight lamp and UV lamp.
Preparation of o-CDs and fabrication of o-CDs based WLED device.
Fitting parameters and average lifetime of o-CDs.
Comparison of the emission wavelength of CDs and photoelectric parameters of warm WLEDs based on these CDs.

row.entry: Driven current or voltage or power

head.ce:title: Orange-emissive carbon dot phosphors for warm white light-emitting diodes with high color rendering index

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan, 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan, China, 030024

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Carbon Materials, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of New Carbon Materials, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, China, 030024

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Orange-emissive carbon dot (o-CDs) phosphors were prepared to reduce the correlated color temperature and increase the color rendering index of carbon dots (CDs)-based white light-emitting diode (WLED) devices. 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene was selected as the carbon source and N, N-dimethylformamide was used as the nitrogen source to synthesize nitrogen doped o-CDs with wide and double-peak emission spectrum. Warm WLED device was designed by taking o-CDs as phosphors and excited by a UV chip. Results showed that o-CDs have a fluorescence quantum yield of 26.03%, their emission spectrum is wide enough to range from 480 to 680 nm, and show good photo and thermal stability, which is quite suitable for warm WLED phosphors. o-CDs based WLED device emits warm white light with a color coordinate of (0.41, 0.39) and a correlated color temperature of 3330 K. Compared with the current warm WLEDs devices based on CDs, their color rendering index reaches a high level, which is 91. This study provides a new idea for broadening the application of CDs in warm WLEDs that used in indoor lighting.

Orange-emissive carbon dot (o-CDs) phosphors were prepared to fabricate warm white light-emitting diode (WLED) devices with high color rendering index.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a new generating green solid-state light source, white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) are widely applied in the fields of lighting, backlight, and display owing to their low energy consumption, high light efficiency, long life, no radiation, etc [1,2]. In current market, the WLED devices produced by combining a blue LED chip and yellow phosphors generally have a correlated color temperature (CCT) of above 6000 K and the light color is bluish, which is benefit for outdoor lighting [3,4]. However, as for indoor lighting, warm white light spectrum with a reddish color and a CCT around 3000 K is strongly desirable to keep the human eyes from staving off eye fatigue [5]. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop warm WLED devices for indoor health lighting.

At present, warm WLED devices based on phosphors such as rare earth phosphors [6], colloidal quantum dots [7], and carbon dots (CDs) [8–11] have been widely reported. Among them, CDs with excellent photoluminescence properties, good photostability, heat stability, and low toxicity stand out [12–15]. Nevertheless, the application of CDs in warm WLEDs still has some limitations: 1) most of CDs emit blue light at a wavelength of around 440 nm, leading that WLEDs have a CCT above 5000 K with cool white light; 2) owing to the narrow emission spectrum of CDs, the color rendering index (CRI) of WLED is low (less than 85), giving rise to great color differences [16,17]. These factors limit the further application of CDs in warm WLED devices.

Recently, researchers have made great efforts to improve CCT and CRI of CDs-based warm WLED devices. For example, Du et al. synthesized green light emissive fluorescent CDs and applied them to warm WLEDs as phosphors [18]. The obtained devices have a color coordinates of (0.39, 0.47) and CCT of 4323 K. Liu et al. excite yellow-emissive CDs phosphors by a 365 nm ultraviolet light to fabricate warm WLED devices, and their CCT was reduced to 1596 K [19]. According to these researches, it can be found that as the emission spectra of CDs is narrow and the emission wavelength is not long enough (less than 500 nm), the CRI of warm WLEDs still leave something to de desired. On this basis, Meng et al. prepared warm WLED devices by using CDs with ultra-wide emission spectrum CDs, and obtained the devices with a CCT of 3032 K and a CRI of 91 [20]. These results demonstrate that red shifting the emission wavelength of CDs and widening their emission spectrum are expected to fabricate warm WLED devices with low CCT and high CRI.

Herein, this work is aims to further widen the emission spectrum and realize the redshift of the emission wavelength of CDs, obtaining warm WLED devices with low CCT and high CRI. In this work, 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene with a large surface electron cloud density and sp2 conjugate size of was used as a carbon source, N, N-dimethylformamide was used as a nitrogen source, and ethanol was used as a solvent to synthesize nitrogen doped orange-emissive CDs (o-CDs) with a wide emission spectrum (as shown in Scheme 1 ). As a single-matrix phosphor, o-CDs were excited by ultraviolet chip to prepare WLED devices. The devices have a color coordinates of (0.41, 0.39) and CCT of 3330 K, and the CRI is as high as 91, which is suitable for warm white light occasions and indoor lighting.

2, 7-Dihydroxynaphthalene (C10H6(OH)2) was purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, C3H7NO), silane coupling agent (KH-792) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. 365 nm UV chip (working voltage 3.0–3.6 V) was produced by Shenzhen Guanghuashi Technology Co., Ltd. Ultra-pure water (H2O) was obtained from our laboratory.

0.4 g of 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene were put into a beaker and then 60 μL of DMF dissolved in 30 mL of ethanol were added. The beaker was sonicated for 15 min until the solutes were completely dissolved. After that, the solution was transferred into a high-pressure solvothermal reactor and kept at 180 °C for 14 h, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was firstly filtered by 0.22 μm needle membrane to remove impurities, and then dialyzed in water through a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 48 h for further purification. Subsequently, the solution in the dialysis bag was concentrated by a rotary evaporator and freeze-dried to obtain solid powders, which are o-CDs.
The optical properties of o-CDs were analyzed by Ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis), photoluminescence (PL) and transient fluorescence.

The o-CDs solution was diluted with ethanol in a quartz cuvette, and then put into a dark box ultraviolet analyzer (ZF-20D, Shanghai Jihui scientific analysis instrument Co., Ltd.). Their fluorescence was observed at 365 nm UV irradiation, and recorded by photograph. The UV–Vis and PL spectra of o-CDs were measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi high tech company, Japan) and fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax–4, Horiba, France). The transient fluorescence spectrometer (F980, Edinburgh, UK) was used to analyze the performance of the fluorescence decay to obtain the fluorescence lifetime of o-CDs.

The quantum yield (QY) of o-CDs was determined by relative method. Rhodamine B was chosen as a reference material and ethanol was chosen as the solvent [18]. The QY of Rhodamine B was 56% when the excited wavelength is 510 nm. Firstly, UV–Vis was used to measure the absorbance of Rhodamine B solution and o-CDs solution at 510 nm. To avoid the influence of internal filtration, all the absorbance values were controlled less than 0.1. The PL spectra of four standard solutions and o-CDs solutions with different absorbance were measured. Fluorescence integral intensity in the emission wavelength range of 530–700 nm was calculated and recorded. Taking the absorbance as the abscissa and the fluorescence integral intensity as the ordinate respectively, a graph was draw to calculate the slope. Then QY of o-CDs is calculated according to the following formula:
Q =
Q st
×
(
K x
K st
)
×
(
n x
n st
)
2
where Q is QY, K is the slope, and n is the refractive index of the solution. The subscript x and st represent the test sample and the standard, respectively.

The anti-UV stability test was carried out by putting the o-CDs solution under UV light (10 W, λex = 365 nm) for 60 min continuously and measuring the change of fluorescence intensity with radiation time. The distance between samples and UV light is 5 cm.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and High-resolution TEM (HRTEM, JEM 2100, JEOL, Japan) were used to characterize the morphology, microstructure and particle size of o-CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, TENSOR 27, Germany BRUKER) was used to characterize the surface functional groups and chemical bonds of o-CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, PHI5000 Versa Probe type, Japanese ULVAC-PHI) and elemental analyzer (EA, Vario EL/micro cube type, German EA) were used to analyze the element content of the product. A thermogravimetric analyzer (TG, 209F3, NETZSCH, Germany) was used to study the thermal stability of the o-CDs phosphor, and the test conditions were under nitrogen atmosphere and a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of o-CDs based WLED devices
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mg of o-CDs powder was dispersed in a mixture of 10 mL of KH-792 and deionized water (1:1 by volume), and sonicated for 3 min 30 μL of o-CDs dispersion was dropped into the optical lens, and dried in an oven at 80 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the cup-shaped optical lens coated with o-CDs was placed on the bottom of the UV chip to prepare o-CDs based WLED devices. A spectral scanning colorimeter (PR-655 Spectra Scan type, USA) was used to measure and analyze the optical properties of the prepared WLED device, including the emission spectrum, color coordinates, CCT, and CRI. The scanning wavelength range was 380–780 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and elemental composition of o-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To clear their luminescence mechanism, the functional groups and elemental composition on the surface of o-CDs were analyzed by FTIR analysis. Before that FTIR spectra of o-CDs obtained before and after dialysis were compared to determine the necessity for purification of dialysis, as shown in Fig. 1 . Before dialysis, the vibrational absorption peaks at 3309, 1629, 1197, and 1292 cm−1 are assigned to the groups of –OH, CO, C–O, and C–O–C, respectively, indicating that o-CDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups [21,22]. The wide absorption band at 3309 cm−1 demonstrates that the surface of o-CDs has abundant hydroxyl groups, which endows their high polarity and dispersity in the water solution [23]. The weak absorption peaks at 2929 and 2870 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration absorption peaks of –CH2– and –CH3. The peaks at 3230 and 1516 cm−1 are observed due to the vibrational absorption peaks of N–H and C–N, respectively. After dialysis, almost all peaks coincide with those before dialysis, which demonstrating the raw materials were completely reacted and the filtration can remove the impurities. The PL measurements were also compared in the supplemental data (Fig. S1 presented in the Supplemental Materials) and confirmed the above results. Therefore, all the characterizations below in this work were carried out on the o-CDs without dialysis.

Moreover, the existence of N–H and C–N in FTIR spectra indicate that DMF participated in the synthesis of o-CDs as a nitrogen source [24]. According to the electronegativity difference between nitrogen atom (3.04) and carbon atom (2.55), it can be known that nitrogen doping can change the electron cloud density of the surface carbon layer and promote radiative recombination. Therefore, the incorporation of nitrogen atoms injects excessive free electrons into CDs, changes the internal electronic environment, and improves the photoluminescence performance. Zhang et al. have adjusted the fluorescence emission of CDs from blue to yellow by doping nitrogen [25]. They found that the emission wavelength of CDs gradually red shifted as the proportion of nitrogen doping increased, which is induced by the new emission centers after nitrogen doping. These results illustrate that nitrogen doping in o-CDs in this work could help the red shift of emission wavelength.

The composition of o-CDs was further confirmed by XPS (Fig. 2 ). Full scans by XPS (Fig. 2a) shows the presence of C1s, N1s, and O1s with peaks located at 281, 398, and 529 eV, respectively. High-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2b) show the existence of graphene sp2 C at 284.5 eV and sp3 C at 285.1 and 286.3 eV, corresponding to the groups of CC/C–C, C–N and CO [21]. The high-resolution N1s (Fig. 2c) spectra reveal two different types of nitrogen doping: pyrrolic nitrogen at about 399.6 eV and graphitic nitrogen at about 401.0 eV. The existence of CO at 531.8 eV and C–OH/C–O–C at 532.9 eV are verified by the high-resolution O1s (Fig. 2d) spectra [26,27]. These results are consistent with the FTIR spectroscopy analysis, demonstrating the diversity of functional groups on the surface of o-CDs.

Moreover, the content of each element was further determined by the elemental analysis (Table 1 ). It can be seen that 19.83% of oxygen and 1.16% of nitrogen were doped in o-CDs. All these results demonstrate that various oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of o-CDs can benefit to generate multiple fluorescent emission centers and broaden the emission spectrum of o-CDs.

Based on these results, a probable schematic illustration for synthesis of o-CDs was drawn: under solvothermal conditions, N, N-dimethylformamide was firstly decomposed to dimethylamine, and then 2, 7-Dihydroxynaphthalene and dimethylamine molecules undergo dehydration and condensation at initial reaction stage [28]. With the extension of reaction time, products are further polymerized and carbonized to form o-CDs. The obtained o-CDs have a nucleus of nitrogen heterocycle and oxygen heterocycle, and have abundant functional groups [29], including –OH, CO and N–H, as shown in Fig. 3 .

TEM and HRTEM were measured to analysis the morphology and size distribution of o-CDs, as shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen that o-CDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a narrow and uniform size distribution. The average particle size is 2.93 nm. Fig. 4b showed that o-CDs have obvious lattice fringes, and the lattice spacing is calculated to be 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the facet of graphitic carbon (100) [30].

The fluorescence performance of o-CDs was analyzed by UV–Vis and PL spectra. It can be seen from Fig. 5 a that o-CDs have three strong absorption peaks at 239, 325, and 553 nm. The absorption peaks at 239 and 325 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transitions of CO, while the absorption peak at 553 nm indicates that o-CDs have a greater degree of sp2 conjugation [31]. Fig. 5b is the PL spectra of o-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. It shows o-CDs exhibit double-peak emission at 531 and 584 nm, respectively. With the increase of the excitation wavelength, the intensity of the emission peak at 531 nm gradually increases, and reaches the maximum when the excitation wavelength is 460 nm. When the excitation wavelength continuously increases, the emission intensity decreases, and the emission peak always keeps at 531 nm. Similarly, the emission intensity of the excitation peak at 584 nm also shows a trend of initially increasing and then decreasing with the increase of the excitation wavelength. The emission intensity reaches the maximum at the excitation of 540 nm. It illustrates that both the emission at 531 and 584 nm have excitation independent properties. It is generally believed that the excitation-independent behavior of CDs depends on the distribution of surface states [32]. This excited independent emission behavior indicates that the surface states and size of o-CDs are quite uniform. The double-peak emission of o-CDs can be attributed to two kinds of emission centers that may originate from their surface states or eigenstate, which will discuss below.

Relative method was used to measure the QY of o-CDs. Rhodamine B was selected as the standard material and its QY was 56% excited at 510 nm. The test results are shown in Fig. 6 a. After calculation, the QY of o-CDs was 26.03%, which is higher than that of most reported orange-emissive CDs.

To further explore the fluorescence properties of o-CDs, the fluorescence lifetime was measured at 375 nm by the multi-dimensional time-correlated single photon counting method, as shown in Fig. 6b. The PL decay curve of o-CDs could be well fitted with a double-exponential decay [18], as formula (1):
(1)
Y
( t )
=
B 1
exp
(
−
τ
τ 1
)
+
B 2
exp
(
−
τ
τ 2
)
where B1 and B2 are the pre-exponential factors for the time-resolved decay lifetimes of τ1 and τ2, respectively. The average life (τav) can be calculated by the following formula (2) or (3):
(2)
τ av
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
(3)
τ av
=
α 1
τ 1
+
α 2
τ 2
where α1 and α2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetimes of τ1 and τ2, respectively.

Fitting parameters and average lifetime of o-CDs are listed in Table 2 . The average fluorescence lifetime is 8.98 ns, and τ2 (long-life component) is 88.75%. It demonstrates long-life component plays a major role in the fluorescence lifetime of o-CDs. Although several groups have reported double-peak emission CDs and paid more attention to their emission centers in recent years, their luminescence mechanism is still unclear. Up to 2019, Zhu et al. completely confirmed that the typical multiemissions originated from band gap state (carbon core), surface defect state, and molecular state by means of luminescence spectra, time-resolved emission spectroscopy, FL decay, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, and so forth [33]. Specifically, in this work, the double-peak emission of o-CDs can be attributed to two kinds of emission centers that may come from surface state or carbon-core state. According to previous work, the fluorescence derived from surface states has a longer lifetime, while the fluorescence originated from carbon-core state generally shows a shorter lifetime [34–36]. Therefore, the dominant of long-life component in o-CDs revealed that the fluorescence decay kinetics is mainly caused by their surface states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of o-CDs in warm WLED

ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the feasibility of o-CDs as a single matrix phosphor for warm WLEDs, the emission spectrum of o-CDs solutions excited at 360 nm were examined [10]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the emission spectrum of o-CDs is divided into six colors, including purple, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red. Among them, the proportion of blue-red light is as high as 82.05%, which is higher than 67.4% of the blue-red light of CDs reported in the literature [9]. This may be attributed to various oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups on the surface of o-CDs, which introduce a variety of energy levels related to O and N. Therefore, the electronic transition forms are diversified and the emission spectra are widened, which makes o-CDs quite suitable for the preparation of warm WLEDs as single phosphor.

The stability of o-CDs against the photobleaching experiment was carried out under UV light irradiation for 60 min with a wavelength of 365 nm [37,38]. The fluorescence intensity of o-CDs solution was measured every 5 min interval. As shown in the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 8 ), the fluorescence intensity of the o-CDs solution continually decreased but not that much significant amount. After 60 min of UV radiation, the fluorescence intensity of the o-CDs solution can still maintain at 95.3% of the initial solution, indicating that o-CDs have good stability against UV light [18]. The obtained experimental results suggest that the o-CDs is effective against fluorescence photobleaching and suitable for long life WLED applications.

Since the working temperature of the LED device is around 120 °C, the phosphor is required to have good thermal stability [26]. Fig. 9 is the thermogravimetric curve of o-CDs under nitrogen atmosphere. It can be seen that o-CDs have almost no weight loss at 100–200 °C, indicating that o-CDs have good thermal stability in the working temperature. They begin to lose weight after 200 °C, and have a fast weight loss rate at 200–280 °C, which may be due to the dehydration carbonization of the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of o-CDs. As the temperature continually increases, little weight was lost. To further study the thermal stability of o-CDs, they were kept at 120 °C for 1 h in a blast drying oven, and then the emission spectra of o-CDs ethanol solution under 540 nm excitation of o-CDs before and after heat treatment were measured (as shown in Fig. 8b). It can be seen that after heat treated at 120 °C, the luminescent properties of o-CDs have a certain degree of attenuation (15.7%), showing good thermal stability. These results demonstrate that the thermal stability of o-CDs has met the requirements of LED phosphors.

WLED devices were obtained by dispersing o-CDs in KH-792 and combining them with an ultraviolet chip. As shown in Fig. 10 , the WLEDs can emit bright white light, indicating that KH-792 can effectively suppress fluorescence quenching caused by o-CDs [11]. Driven by a voltage of 3.4 V, the emission spectrum consists of three parts: the emission peak of the residual UV chip is before 400 nm, the defect peak of the device is at around 530 nm and the emission peak of the o-CDs is at around 600 nm.

It is worthy note that compared with the emission of o-CDs dispersed in ethanol solution, a slightly red shift of the emission peak takes place (Fig. 10a). To study this phenomenon, the effects of KH-792 on the luminescent properties of the o-CDs were investigated. Firstly, the emission spectra of 10 μg/mL of o-CDs with/without KH-792 and 100 μg/mL of o-CDs in ethanol solution were measured, as shown Fig. 11 a (the amount of KH-792 was the same as the fabrication of WLED devices). It can be seen that KH-792 doesn't affect the luminescent properties of the o-CDs in solution, while self-absorption caused by high concentration of o-CDs can lead a little red shift of emission spectra. Further, the photoluminescence properties of the o-CDs powders were investigated by daylight lamp and UV lamp at 365 nm, as shown in Fig. 11b. It shows that no obvious fluorescence from o-CDs powder is observed when illuminated by UV light as a result of fluorescence quenching in the solid state. A conclusion can be drawn that the introduction of KH-792 can effectively suppress fluorescence quenching. In our previous work, KH-792 has been confirmed as the precursor simultaneously for silica matrix as disperse agent and for silanol as film forming agent [39]. CD/silica composites were formed by direct injection of KH-792 into a CD aqueous solution. In this way, the silica matrix was formed fast and o-CDs were embedded into silica by physical doping between o-CDs and silica, thus preventing their self-quenching.

Moreover, the emission spectrum of o-CDs covers the entire visible light region, and the color coordinates of the obtained WLED are (0.41, 0.39), the CCK is 3330 K, and the CRI is as high as 91, which is even comparable to some state of the art warm WLEDs based on CDs, especially for their high CRI (Table 3 ). It indicates that as a light source the o-CDs based warm WLED can more realistically reflect the color of objects and is suitable for indoor lighting applications [20].

To reduce the CCK and increase the CRI of CDs-based warm WLED devices, an orange-emitting CDs phosphor with a wide emission spectrum was prepared in this work. Using 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene as the carbon source, DMF as the nitrogen source, and ethanol as the solvent, o-CDs riched in oxygen and nitrogen functional groups were synthesized. These functional groups can provide multiple fluorescent emission centers for o-CDs and red-shift of their emission wavelength. Results show that two emission peaks of o-CDs appear at 531 and 584 nm with an independent excitation property and the spectral range covers from 480 to 680 nm. Good stability and the high proportion of blue-red light made o-CDs are suitable for the application of single phosphors towards warm WLED. Combined with the UV chip, the obtained WLED emits warm white light with color coordinates (0.41, 0.39) and CCK of 3330 K. The CRI of WLED is up to 91, which is suitable for warm white light applications such as indoor lighting.
Lin Chen: Writing - original draft preparation. Jingxia Zheng: Writing - review & editing. Qin Du: Data curation. Yongzhen Yang: Conceptualization. Xuguang Liu: Methodology. Bingshe Xu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803148, 51972221, U1710117 and U1607120), Shanxi Provincial Youth Fund Project (201801D221180, 201901D211501), Shanxi Provincial Excellent Talents Science and Technology Innovation Project (201805D211001) and Shanxi Scholarship Council of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2020.110346.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel multi-center activated single-component white light-emitting phosphor for deep UV chip-based high color-rendering WLEDs
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. W. Zhang, J. H. Li, N. Yang, Q.Y. Liang, Y. Q. Xu, S. T. Fu, J. Yan, J. B. Zhou, J. X. Shi, M. M. Wu. A novel multi-center activated single-component white light-emitting phosphor for deep UV chip-based high color-rendering WLEDs, Chem. Eng. J. 390 (2020) 124601.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nano wave plates structuring and index matching in transparent hydroxyapatite-YAG: Ce composite ceramics for high luminous efficiency white light-emitting diodes
Adv. Mater.
